transcript:
So, welcome guys, welcome to this talk. Um, I'm Sebastian, I'm the co-founder of Neodyme, leading security firm here in the Solana ecosystem. And today, we will talk about control, especially about who controls Solana's funds. There's this saying in more conspiracy-minded communities: follow the money. Today we are going to follow the seeds to find the money.

So, this was our starting question. As security researchers, we want to work efficiently, so if the top protocols by TVL are secure, most funds are secure. And indeed, we again got a Pareto coefficient here or I don't know how you call it, this 80/20 stuff. Indeed, 80% of the funds in Solana are locked up in the top seven programs. But how do we actually find out what are the richest protocols on Solana? Is it Kamino? Is it Jupiter? Is it Drift? They all got a lot of value locked. Or is it Squads?

Take a minute, pause, and think about it yourself. Um, most of you would probably just go to some DeFi aggregator and check. But stay tuned, the data may not be that correct there.

So, how do we find it out trustless? To find this out, we have to understand on a technical level how smart contracts can control Solana funds. In Solana, we got the SPL Token Program where all token accounts are stored. Those token accounts have a mint: USDC, wrapped SOL, you name it. An amount: how much of that mint do we have? And most importantly, they also have an owner. This owner is a Pubkey and it can be three types of Pubkeys. It could be a normal keypair, as you have it on your hardware wallet, on your hot wallet. It could be a seeded address, that's a bit of a lesser-known feature, think of it as PDAs for keypairs. Or it could be a PDA, a Program Derived Address. And if a token account is owned by such a PDA, it can control its funds. But what exactly are those PDAs?

The goal was quite simple. We want smart contracts to sign for an address. So we can enable cross-program interactions, etc. And the problem is also quite obvious: the program cannot have a normal keypair, as I would do, because I would have to upload the keypair up to chain and that kind of defeats the purpose of a private key. So the idea is we somehow allow smart contracts to pseudo-sign by deriving addresses from the program ID which they then can invoke and the runtime counts it as a signature.

On a technical level, this is quite easy. We got two inputs. We got the seeds. This is just an array of an array of bytes, so it can be quite everything. And the program ID, so the program that is signing now. And to find a Program Derived Address, we just take the hash of the seeds, the program ID, and a so-called PDA marker, that is just the string "ProgramDerivedAddress". And this hash is a valid PDA if it does not lie on the edwards curve which you see on the right. Because otherwise if it lies on that curve, uh, there could be a private key associated with it. It would be still quite hard to find, but better be safe than sorry.

So this hash is quite simple. There is actually once was a funny loss of funds bug on Solana, uh, with this because the hash domain, collision domain of those seeded addresses which I talked about before collided with the hash domain of PDAs. I think we wrote a blog post about it. If you Google PDA Neodyme Solana, you will find it. But sorry for the derail.

Exactly. So now we know how Program Derived Addresses function. But we want to check which funds are controlled by which smart contract. So what do we need? We need token accounts, so we know where all the money lies on the blockchain. We need those PDA derivations and we need token prices.

Token accounts are easy. Just parse some snapshot ledger using Agave ledger tool or something else and extract all the token accounts, put them into a database, done.

Next, prices. They are also quite easy to get, just use some API I wrote. I later found out that it isn't that easy because pricing of tokens is quite hard. You got a lot of wash-traded memecoins, etc. But you can filter that, it's doable.

The really hard part is getting those PDAs. Because they aren't materialized anywhere in the ledger. This information of PDAs on Solana is only available at the runtime level. So if you look in the Solana Explorer, you will see a Pubkey. You will also know that it's a PDA because this Pubkey doesn't lie on the edwards curve. But you won't know which program can sign for this Pubkey and you also don't know how it's derived by its seeds. And there has not been a public database yet. Uh, so we set out to build it.

How did we do that? Well, it isn't that complicated actually. We just need to patch the runtime of the Solana validator. Most programs use a syscall, or two syscalls to be specific, to calculate those PDAs: `sol_try_find_program_address` and `sol_create_program_address`. And they offload those hash calculations to the runtime because it's cheaper to do it that way than to do it in eBPF.

And luckily we already had a patch-ready emulation, uh, of all Solana transactions. This serves as the backend of Riverguard. It's a tool we built two years ago which actually scans every live transaction that is happening on chain right now. And, uh, it mutates it according to known attack rules. Uh, and we check if, like some basic, uh, checks like account owner check, signer checks, self-transfers are possible. You can check it out, it's free of charge, everything. Um, but it was quite lucky for this use case because we could quite easy patch the client.

And then we just pushed the PDA seeds into a database. And now we got a database of all PDAs and their program derivations live. You can scan the code if you want. Um, we launched this database public so you can access it all the time. You can just put in the PDA and, uh, you will see its derivations. It's quite cool.

And we also built the teaser I showed you at the front: which program controls most of the funds. Are you ready?

So, it's actually Squads. Most DeFi aggregators don't care about Squads at all because it doesn't earn money, or not that much. But it has the most funds locked in all Solana smart contracts. Next up is Kamino Lend, then Jupiter Perpetual, Squads v3. Um, and it's quite interesting. If you click on that on the website, you will get to a next view... Oh, congrats to everybody who did it right.

Uh, you will get to the next view and you will see in which token mints are those funds actually stored. And if you click for example on USDC, you will actually get to the view where you see: this token account is owned by that address which is derived by this seeds. And this is super useful. For example, the first, we can already see by the seeds that this program apparently is a multisig. Next one is probably the multisig ID. After that the vault. So we exactly see how does a program function even if we don't know the source code of it at all.

Some interesting numbers, uh, to wrap it up. Um, I thought it would be interesting to share how much of a mint supply is actually locked in contracts, uh, in contracts in contrast to, uh, it's held in normal wallets. Um, so most value locked, uh, in PDAs is by USDC and it's about 14.6% of the supply. Next up by Total Value Locked is Pump and about 50% of all Pump tokens are locked in contracts. And for wrapped SOL for example, it's nearly 73% of all those tokens which are held in smart contracts and not in any wallets.

Uh, in total we roughly have 25 million different PDA-owned token accounts, so quite a number. And our database currently has about 104 million unique PDAs stored. The top program by most PDAs owned is the Jito Tip Distribution Program with 27 million PDAs. This database isn't complete because we don't have historic data. As I showed you, it's a dynamic process of getting those PDAs. Um, but it should have the most common and most recently PDAs in there and it's still growing quite a lot.

That's it for now. Thank you for listening. Follow on socials, etc. And hit us up, I will be back in the hallway. Thank you.

# Bringing Cross-Chain Wallets Into Aptos: X-Chain Accounts
[
![Aptos Labs](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*oFLHYpAFW3E9-ZMooaqahQ.jpeg)
](https://aptoslabs.medium.com/@aptoslabs?source=post_page---byline--b5ac60995143---------------------------------------)

·Nov 5, 2025
[
](https://aptoslabs.medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https://medium.com/_/vote/aptoslabs/b5ac60995143&operation=register&redirect=https://medium.com/aptoslabs/bringing-cross-chain-wallets-into-aptos-x-chain-accounts-b5ac60995143&user=Aptos+Labs&userId=70211828fe2e&source=---header_actions--b5ac60995143---------------------clap_footer------------------)

Listen
Share
By Maayan Savir
Press enter or click to view image in full size
![]()
*TL;DR: Developers can now onboard Ethereum and Solana users directly into their Aptos dApps: no new wallets, seed phrases, or bridges required. With AIP-121 and AIP-122, Aptos natively supports authentication from external wallets like MetaMask and Phantom.*
The blockchain landscape has never been more fragmented. In 2025, more than $2 trillion in digital assets were distributed across over 40 active L1 and L2 networks. Yet every ecosystem remains a silo, each with its own wallets, key formats, and onboarding flows.
Over the past year, the Aptos ecosystem has expanded into a global trading engine, connecting DEXs, payments, and real-world assets into one high-throughput, low-latency network.
Accessibility is the next step for scale.
Thanks to AIP-113, Aptos can support native authentication standards from other ecosystems directly on-chain. Dev

sources.title: Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
sources.snippet:
Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
[](https://chainwire.org/)
[Log In](https://app.chainwire.org/login)
# Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More - Chainwire
* [Home](https://chainwire.org/)
* [Newsroom](https://chainwire.org/category/newsroom/)
* Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More
![](https://chainwire.org/wp-content/smush-webp/2025/12/Group_4138_1764775174e7nrJV5R9j.jpg.webp)
**04**Dec
## Ika Announces EdDSA Signatures, Expanding Native Support to Solana, Zcash, Cardano and More
**Zug, Switzerland, December 4th, 2025, Chainwire**
[Ika](https://ika.xyz/), the fastest parallel MPC network, today announced a mainnet upgrade that enables**EdDSA signatures directly from dWallets**, significantly expanding Ika’s native cross-chain coverage to include**Solana, Zcash, Cardano, Stellar, Near**, and other EdDSA-based ecosystems.
This upgrade extends Ika’s core promise:**natively control accounts and assets across chains without bridges, wrapping, custodians, or trusted signers**, now across a broader set of networks where EdDSA is the standard.
> “EdDSA support is a major expansion of what dWallets can control natively,” said **> Omer Sadika
**> , Co-Founder of Ika. “It brings zero-trust, programmable signing to some of the most important ecosystems in crypto, so developers can orchestrate real cross-chain actions with real native assets, governed by Ika dWallet technology.”
**What This Upgrade Enables**
Until now, Ika’s dWallets provided zero-trust signing and programmable control for major ecosystems, including Bitcoin and EVM-compatible networks with ECDSA signatures. With EdDSA enabled at the dWallet signing layer, builders can now create applications that:
* **Control Solana accounts natively**, enabling cross-chain trading, vaults, payments, and automation without wrapped assets.
* **Coordinate Zcash-native actions**under programmable dWallet policies, unlocking zero-trust custody and interoperability for the most prominent privacy-preserving cryptocurrency.
* **Manage Cardano, Stellar, Near accounts**with smart contract-governed access control, enabling organizational treasury workflows and institutional-grade policy enforcement.
In short:**dWallets become natively multi-ecosystem signers**, and enable a coordination layer for policy, composability, and execution logic.
**Native Cross-Chain Control**
Most cross-chain systems rely on bridges, message relays, wrappers, or trusted intermediaries. These approaches introduce risk (custody, exploits, governance capture) and limit composability.
Ika’s model is different:**no bridging of assets is required**. Instead, Ika enables**native signing**via dWallets, where transaction authorization is always enforced under zero-trust assumptions.
With EdDSA enabled, the same architecture now extends to chains where ECDSA is not supported and EdDSA has been adopted instead, dramatically expanding the surface area of what Ika dWallets can safely and directly orchestrate.
**How EdDSA Works in Ika’s Zero-Trust Model**
Ika’s security model is built on its**2PC-MPC**cryptographic scheme.
dWallet signing splits authorization into two independent components:
* **User share:**held and authorized locally by the user
* **Network share:**operated by Ika’s decentralized network of MPC nodes
Both are required to produce a valid signature, and the full private key is never reconstructed. With this mainnet upgrade, that same model now supports**EdDSA signing**, bringing the benefits of Ika’s architecture (zero-trust, decentralization, scalability, sub-second performance, and programmable policy enforcement) to a wider set of chains.
**Why It Matters for Builders and the Ecosystem**
This upgrade expands the design space for applications that are only possible with Ika, including:
**Universal accounts across ecosystems**
One programmab

# Major Exchange Solana Breach Shakes 2025 Crypto Market
**[MEXC Blog](https://blog.mexc.com/author/shaunt/)**November 27, 2025**[Crypto News & Updates](https://blog.mexc.com/category/news/)
## Overview: Large Solana-Based Withdrawal Detected
On 27 November 2025, a prominent South Korean cryptocurrency exchange detected unauthorized withdrawals of Solana-based assets totaling approximately 54 billion KRW (about $36 million). The irregular outflow included native SOL and a range of tokens from the Solana ecosystem. The exchange immediately suspended Solana network deposits and withdrawals and initiated emergency inspections to contain the incident.
![South Korean exchange flags $36M Solana withdrawals, suspends deposits](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20210%20140%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)
### Affected assets and scope
* SOL (Solana native token)
* USDC (Solana-based stablecoin)
* Several Solana-native tokens across DeFi and meme markets
* Stolen funds were transferred to external, unidentified wallets
The exchange disclosed wallet addresses linked to the irregular withdrawals as part of its rapid response and forensic efforts. Early analysis indicates the incident was concentrated on assets held on the Solana network, suggesting a vectored compromise rather than a cross-chain exploit.
## Immediate Response and Containment Measures
Following detection, the exchange enacted multiple containment steps:
* Suspension of all Solana network deposits and withdrawals.
* Activation of emergency inspection protocols and forensic investigations.
* Public communication to affected customers with ongoing updates through official channels.
* Disclosure of wallet addresses associated with the outflows to support blockchain tracing efforts.
According to the exchange’s public noti

editorial.headline: Neodyme Unveils 'Follow the Seeds' to Expose Solana’s True TVL Controller
editorial.standfirst: Security firm releases a public database of 104 million Program Derived Addresses, revealing that multisig protocol Squads controls more value than any DeFi aggregator suggests.
key_takeaways: Squads identified as the top protocol by value locked, surpassing Kamino and Jupiter.
Neodyme launched a public database tracking 104 million unique PDAs and their derivation seeds.
73% of all Wrapped SOL and 50% of Pump tokens are locked in smart contracts rather than user wallets.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
In a move that redefines how institutional investors should analyze on-chain risk, security firm **Neodyme** has released a comprehensive database of Solana Program Derived Addresses (PDAs), effectively mapping the "dark matter" of the ecosystem's Total Value Locked (TVL). While standard DeFi aggregators track yield-generating protocols, they often miss the underlying custodial infrastructure. Neodyme’s analysis reveals that **Squads**, a multisig standard often ignored by retail-focused dashboards, actually holds the most value on the network—surpassing high-profile DeFi giants like **Kamino** and **Jupiter**.

The discrepancy stems from the technical opacity of PDAs. Unlike standard public keys, PDAs are not materialized in the ledger until runtime, making them invisible to traditional scraping methods unless the specific derivation seeds are known. To solve this, **Sebastian Fritsch** and the Neodyme team patched the Solana validator runtime—leveraging their existing **Riverguard** security tool—to intercept `sol_try_find_program_address` syscalls. This allowed them to capture the elusive "seeds" (the inputs used to derive an address) in real-time, building a dataset of over 104 million unique addresses that links funds directly to their controlling programs.

The data paints a starkly different picture of the ecosystem's capital structure. Beyond the dominance of Squads, the analysis highlights a massive shift toward programmatic custody: nearly 73% of the entire **Wrapped SOL** supply and 50% of **Pump** tokens are locked within smart contracts rather than held in user wallets. This signals a mature, automated ecosystem where capital is increasingly managed by code rather than manual private key signatures.

For institutional allocators, this tool offers a new layer of due diligence. Investors can now bypass frontend interfaces and verify the custodial hierarchy of a vault directly. By querying the database, a user can confirm if a specific token account is owned by a **Squads** vault, identify the multisig ID, and trace the authority chain back to its source—all without needing access to the project's private source code. This "follow the seeds" approach provides a trustless method to verify that funds are actually controlled by the governance structures promised in marketing decks.
